query: mortality effects of growth hormone replacement therapy in adults with growth hormone deficiency

retrieved_docs.title: HIV-Related Endocrinopathies -- Prognosis -- Growth Hormone Deficiency
retrieved_docs.content: In general, replacing GH in patients with true deficiency is associated with increased lean muscle mass, decreased fat mass, and improved quality of life. Improvement in bone mineral density with GH replacement was noted in men but not women for unclear reasons. [104] Studies of the effect of GH on lipid profiles revealed conflicting results. Improvement in specific cardiac function parameters, such as left ventricular mass and stroke volume, has been demonstrated, but data on mortality is limited. [105]

retrieved_docs.title: HIV-Related Endocrinopathies -- Prognosis -- Growth Hormone Deficiency
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with GH deficiency secondary to hypopituitarism have increased all-cause mortality that persists despite hormone replacement. [106] [107] There are no current studies investigating the treatment of individuals with HIV and biochemical GH deficiency with hormone replacement.

retrieved_docs.title: Short Stature -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: As growth hormone has a permissive role in tissue growth, concerns were raised regarding an increased risk of primary malignancy, malignancy recurrence, or secondary malignancy in cancer survivors. A recent consensus concluded, "Current evidence does not support an association between growth hormone replacement and primary tumor or cancer recurrence. The effect of growth hormone therapy on secondary neoplasia risk is small compared to host- and tumor treatment-related factors. Evidence does not support an association between growth hormone replacement and increased mortality from cancer among growth hormone-deficient childhood cancer survivors. Patients with pituitary tumors or craniopharyngioma remnants receiving growth hormone replacement do not need to be treated or monitored differently from those not receiving growth hormone." [37]

retrieved_docs.title: Short Stature -- Treatment / Management -- Treatment with Human Growth Hormone
retrieved_docs.content: Monitoring and dose adjustment are based primarily on growth response, assessed every 6 to 12 months. Serum IGF-1 levels should also be monitored to evaluate treatment adherence, biological responsiveness, and safety. Target IGF-1 levels are around 0 SDs in GHD and can be titrated up to +2 SDs in non-GHD conditions. Once catch-up growth is achieved, dosing should be adjusted to sustain a growth velocity that tracks with MPH. Thyroid function and cortisol levels should be assessed 3 months after initiating therapy and then annually, to detect the emergence of other pituitary hormone deficiencies and guide appropriate management.

retrieved_docs.title: Short Stature -- Treatment / Management -- Treatment with Human Growth Hormone
retrieved_docs.content: Treatment should be discontinued when height is satisfactory to the patient and family, or when the growth velocity falls below 2 to 2.5 cm/year over at least 6 months. Another indication to stop treatment is attainment of a bone age of 14 years in girls and 16 years in boys.

retrieved_docs.title: Dwarfism -- Treatment Planning
retrieved_docs.content: The child receives daily subcutaneous growth hormone (GH) injections, and are advised to take GH injections regularly, preferably within 1 hour of sleep every night, at a specific time, and not to miss more than one dose a month. The patient usually follows-up 2 to 4 times a year. The growth rate is maximum in the first year of treatment, ranging from 8-10cm/year; this is called 'catch-up growth.' The growth rate slows in the next several years, called the 'waning effect.' If the growth rate is slower than expected, it warrants further investigation to rule out other medical conditions like Hypothyroidism or IBD. The treatment continues until the patient reaches her full adult height or reaches full bone maturity or has grown less than 2 cm in the last year.

retrieved_docs.title: HIV-Related Endocrinopathies -- Treatment / Management -- Growth Hormone Deficiency
retrieved_docs.content: The management of GH deficiency due to a primary pituitary or hypothalamic disease requires assessing other pituitary hormones, hormone replacement as needed, and disease-specific treatment. GH replacement is indicated for most patients whose onset of GH deficiency occurs in childhood and persists into adulthood. However, for patients with adult-onset GH deficiency, treatment decisions should be made via shared decision-making while considering the high cost and burden of treatment with relatively modest benefits.

retrieved_docs.title: Hypopituitarism -- Treatment / Management -- GH deficit
retrieved_docs.content: In contrast to the well-defined role of synthetic GH replacement therapy in treating GH deficiency–related short stature in children, the role of GH replacement therapy for adults with GH deficiency has not been firmly established. In pediatric cases, synthetic GH treatments, such as somatotropin, are utilized, and the therapy is adjusted according to insulin-like growth factor 1 (IGF-1) levels. The objective of the treatment is to attain the individual's complete adult stature. Further evaluation is conducted after puberty to determine whether GH replacement therapy should continue until the individual reaches adulthood.

retrieved_docs.title: Short Stature -- Treatment / Management -- Treatment with Human Growth Hormone
retrieved_docs.content: Before the advent of recombinant hGH, treatment for GHD relied on cadaver pituitary-derived hGH, which was limited by scarce supply and the risk of transmitting infections. The introduction of recombinant hGH in 1985 revolutionized therapy by ensuring an unlimited supply for GHD patients and expanding its use to several non-GHD conditions where the benefits outweighed the risks. The U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) first approved recombinant hGH for pediatric GHD, followed in the next 2 decades by approvals for chronic renal insufficiency, Turner syndrome, PWS, children born SGA with poor catch-up growth, ISS, SHOX gene haploinsufficiency, and Noonan syndrome.

retrieved_docs.title: Dwarfism -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: The GH used in treatment is similar to GH released from the pituitary gland and is safe and effective.

retrieved_docs.title: Constitutional Growth Delay -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: It is important to remember that only about 3% of short stature patients have an actual endocrinologic problem that will need further treatment, which is why it is important to take a good history. If there is a concern in growth delay after proper height documentation, the patient needs to be sent for GH levels, IGF-1, and bone age. If this results within normal limits including a delay in bone age that correlates with the height of the patient, most likely it is safe to continue to monitor the patient. If taking this approach, and any of the laboratory workup or bone age are uncertain, or this type of lab workup cannot be done or properly interpreted, a specialist referral is in order. retrieved_docs.title: Dwarfism -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The normal variants of short stature can expect to lead healthy lives. The patients suffering from GHD can benefit from recombinant human growth hormone therapy and generally attain heights similar to their parents, given that the treatment is commenced before puberty. The short stature secondary to genetic and skeletal disorders cannot be reversed and remains a life long issue.

retrieved_docs.title: Short Stature -- Treatment / Management -- Treatment with Human Growth Hormone
retrieved_docs.content: More recently, long-acting growth hormone (LAGH) formulations have been introduced for the treatment of GHD. Lonapegsomatropin was the first LAGH preparation approved by the U.S. FDA in 2021. (Source: FDA , 2021) Since then, 2 additional LAGH molecules have received approval. These agents have demonstrated noninferior efficacy and comparable safety profiles relative to daily hGH injections. However, LAGH is currently approved only for GHD. The table below summarizes currently available LAGH options across various regions, highlighting their molecular features, branding, and regulatory approvals.

retrieved_docs.title: Short Stature -- Treatment / Management -- Treatment with Human Growth Hormone
retrieved_docs.content: Once GHD is confirmed based on clinical features, anthropometry, and laboratory testing, treatment can begin with a daily subcutaneous dose of approximately 20 to 30 mcg/kg. The Pediatric Endocrine Society recommends a dose range of 22 to 35 mcg/kg/day, while the Growth Hormone Research Society suggests 17 to 35 mcg/kg/day. Daily bedtime subcutaneous injections have traditionally been the standard mode of delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: Short Stature -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: In FSS, setting realistic expectations is essential. Families should be reassured that no underlying pathology is present. Treatment with human growth hormone (hGH) is costly and shows variable outcomes in both familial and nonfamilial ISS. HGH may be offered if the family continues to seek intervention after receiving reassurance and the child’s height remains more than 2.25 SDs below the mean, recognizing that FSS may be classified under ISS. However, the modest height gains typically seen in FSS compared to GHD should be clearly communicated. [28] [29]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2279
retrieved_docs.content: Luteinizing Hormone‑releasing Hormone Agonist is classified as a hormone and related agent.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 670
retrieved_docs.content: Somatropin and somatropin agonists constitute a therapeutic class of growth‑hormone–related agents, and this class includes the specific drugs mecasermin, sermorelin, somapacitan, and tesamorelin. Each of these agents functions as an agonist of the somatropin (human growth hormone) pathway, sharing a common mechanism of stimulating growth‑hormone receptors to promote anabolic and metabolic effects. By being members of the somatropin and somatropin agonists class, mecasermin, sermorelin, somapacitan, and tesamorelin are grouped together for clinical considerations such as indications, dosing strategies, and monitoring, facilitating a unified approach to managing conditions like growth‑hormone deficiency and related disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2470
retrieved_docs.content: Synthetic conjugated estrogens A and synthetic conjugated estrogens B are therapeutic agents that may be used to treat menopausal disorders. Both belong to the class of synthetic conjugated estrogens and are indicated for alleviating the hormonal deficiencies and associated symptoms of menopause, such as vasomotor instability, urogenital atrophy, and bone loss. Their use underscores the role of estrogen replacement therapy in managing the clinical manifestations of menopause.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1420
retrieved_docs.content: Somatropin and somatropin agonists constitute a therapeutic class of synthetic growth‑hormone analogues, and the agents mecasermin, somapacitan and tesamorelin are all members of this class. These three drugs share a common mechanism of activating growth‑hormone receptors to stimulate insulin‑like growth factor‑1 production, thereby promoting growth and metabolic effects. Mecasermin is used primarily for treating growth‑failure conditions such as severe primary IGF‑1 deficiency, while somapacitan provides a long‑acting formulation for adult growth‑hormone deficiency, and tesamorelin is employed to reduce excess abdominal fat in patients with HIV‑associated lipodystrophy. Recognizing that mecasermin, somapacitan and tesamorelin belong to the somatropin and somatropin agonist class helps clinicians select appropriate growth‑hormone therapy based on specific patient needs and the pharmacokinetic profiles of each agent.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 4 -- Community 2772
retrieved_docs.content: Megestrol acetate is classified as a progestin, a synthetic analogue of the natural hormone progesterone, and is used in medical practice for hormone‑related therapies, including certain cancer treatments, hormone replacement, and appetite stimulation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 2 -- Community 564
retrieved_docs.content: Somatropin and somatropin agonists constitute a pharmacologic class that includes mecasermin, sermorelin, somapacitan, and tesamorelin. These agents act as growth‑hormone analogues or agonists and are used in the treatment of growth‑hormone deficiency and related disorders. In addition to its role as a somatropin agonist, sermorelin is also classified among pituitary function test diagnostic agents, a group of compounds employed to evaluate pituitary gland activity. Metyrapon is another member of the pituitary function test diagnostic agents category. The overlap of sermorelin in both the somatropin‑agonist class and the pituitary diagnostic class illustrates how certain drugs can serve dual therapeutic and diagnostic purposes, informing both treatment strategies and endocrine assessment in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2534
retrieved_docs.content: ANABOLIC AGENTS FOR SYSTEMIC USE are classified as synthetic androgens, which are man‑made compounds that mimic the biological activity of natural male sex hormones such as testosterone. As synthetic androgens, these agents are administered systemically to produce anabolic effects—including enhanced protein synthesis, muscle growth, and bone density—and are employed in therapeutic settings such as hormone‑deficient states, certain anemias, and muscle‑wasting disorders. Their synthetic nature also confers potential androgenic side effects, necessitating careful medical oversight during use.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1498
retrieved_docs.content: Oxandrolone, an anabolic androgenic steroid, is contraindicated in patients with hypercalcemia, a disorder marked by elevated serum calcium levels; administering oxandrolone in the setting of hypercalcemia can worsen calcium metabolism disturbances and increase the risk of related complications, so clinicians should avoid prescribing this medication to individuals who have this electrolyte abnormality.

From a pharmacologic perspective, the hormonal contraceptive desogestrel and the agent ethynodioldiacetát are contraindicated in patients with diabetes, indicating the need for careful medication selection in this population. Overall, the interconnectedness of diabetes with metabolic syndrome, neonatal forms, ocular and cardiac complications, limb amputations, specific postoperative necrosis, and drug contraindications emphasizes the disease’s systemic impact and the importance of integrated, multidisciplinary management for affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 4 -- Community 2789
retrieved_docs.content: Estradiol and estrogeny are hormonal agents that may be used to treat menopausal disorders, providing therapeutic options for alleviating the symptoms associated with the menopausal transition. Both substances are forms of estrogen therapy and are considered in clinical management to improve quality of life for patients experiencing menopausal-related changes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 4 -- Community 2832
retrieved_docs.content: The GDC Therapeutic Agent Terminology is a classification framework that includes both Luteinizing Hormone‑releasing Hormone Agonist and Thyroid Extract as constituent concepts. Luteinizing Hormone‑releasing Hormone Agonist refers to synthetic analogues that modulate the hypothalamic‑pituitary‑gonadal axis and are employed in the treatment of hormone‑sensitive conditions such as prostate cancer. Thyroid Extract denotes preparations derived from animal thyroid tissue that are used to replace deficient thyroid hormone in hypothyroidism and related disorders. By grouping these distinct therapeutic categories, the GDC Therapeutic Agent Terminology provides a structured system for organizing and referencing diverse drug classes in clinical practice and research.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 1592
retrieved_docs.content: Parathormon is classified within the functional group “hormones by function” and its disposition is that of a hormone. As a hormone, parathormon produces a specific physiologic effect: it leads to decreased renal Ca++ excretion. This reduction in renal calcium loss is a key component of parathormon’s role in maintaining calcium homeostasis, and recognizing that parathormon belongs to the hormone category and exerts this effect is important for the clinical management of calcium‑related disorders.
